Name: training_free_layer_skipping
Title: PAC-Bayes-Guided Layer Skipping for Training-Free Latency-Critical Inference
Short Hypothesis: A PAC-Bayes-guided layer-skipping mechanism, informed by spectral stability bounds, can achieve training-free, latency-aware inference optimization without compromising model accuracy.
Related Work: Existing work on layer-skipping (e.g., SkipNet, BlockDrop) relies on training-based policies, which introduce overhead and limit deployment flexibility. PAC-Bayes theory has been applied to generalization bounds but not to inference optimization. This proposal uniquely combines PAC-Bayes with spectral stability to derive a training-free, latency-aware layer-skipping framework.
Abstract: We propose a novel framework for training-free, latency-critical inference optimization using PAC-Bayes-guided layer skipping. By leveraging spectral stability bounds, we derive a mechanism that dynamically skips layers during inference to meet strict latency budgets without requiring additional training. Our approach introduces a PAC-Bayes-based uncertainty metric that quantifies the impact of layer skipping on model accuracy, ensuring minimal perturbation to the inference process. Experiments on standard benchmarks demonstrate that our method achieves state-of-the-art throughput-accuracy Pareto frontiers while maintaining computational frugality. This work bridges the gap between theoretical optimization bounds and practical inference efficiency, offering a scalable solution for latency-critical applications.
Experiments: 1. Evaluate the proposed layer-skipping mechanism on CIFAR-10 and ImageNet using ResNet and EfficientNet architectures. Measure throughput (FPS) and accuracy (Top-1/Top-5) under varying latency budgets.

2. Compare against baseline methods (SkipNet, BlockDrop) and training-free alternatives (random skipping, fixed patterns). Use ablation studies to isolate the impact of PAC-Bayes and spectral stability components.
3. Quantify the computational overhead of the proposed method (FLOPs, memory usage) and compare it to training-based approaches.
4. Analyze the spectral stability properties of skipped layers using Jacobian-based metrics and correlate them with accuracy degradation.
5. Test robustness under structured perturbations (e.g., noise, domain shifts) to validate the stability of the proposed framework.
Risk Factors and Limitations: The effectiveness of the PAC-Bayes metric may vary across architectures, limiting generalizability.
Layer skipping could introduce instability in models with tightly coupled layers, requiring additional safeguards.
The method assumes access to spectral stability metrics during inference, which may not always be feasible in resource-constrained environments.
The framework may struggle with extremely low-latency budgets where skipping layers significantly degrades accuracy.
